title: Chiffon cake
subtitle: The American oil-and-egg-white cake with a feather-light crumb
excerpt: A tall, cloud-light American cake made with oil instead of butter, leavened with baking powder and stiff egg whites. Baked in a tube pan and cooled upside-down, it has a tender, moist crumb unlike any butter cake.
sourceNotes: The chiffon cake was invented by Harry Baker, a California insurance salesman, in 1927 and kept secret for two decades before being sold to General Mills and published in Better Homes and Gardens magazine in 1948, making it effectively public domain recipe knowledge. The method of folding stiff egg whites into an oil-based batter to produce a cake lighter than a butter cake and moister than an angel food cake was Baker's innovation. General Mills, Betty Crocker Chiffon Cake Recipes (1948), is the founding published source.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze undecorated slices. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well at room temperature for three days, covered loosely.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage of egg white whisking at which the foam stands in firm, upright peaks when the whisk is lifted. The whites hold their shape without drooping.

content.text: Chiffon cake uses oil instead of butter and relies on folded-in egg whites for its rise. The oil keeps the crumb moist even when cold — butter-based cakes firm up in the fridge; chiffon stays soft. The egg whites, whisked to
and folded in at the end, give it an extraordinary lightness. The result sits somewhere between a génoise and an angel food cake: richer than the latter, airier than the former.

The cake must cool upside-down in the tin — this is not optional. Chiffon cake is too light and fragile when hot to support its own weight; inverting it on a wire rack while still warm prevents it collapsing as it cools. Most tube or bundt tins have feet or a central tube tall enough to rest on a rack. If yours does not, balance the upside-down tin on three cups of equal height.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 130 g for the batter, 70 g for the whites
half a tsp; stabilises the whites

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan (180°C conventional). Do not grease the tin — the batter must grip the sides to rise. Whisk {{plain-flour}}, 130 g of {{caster-sugar}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together. Make a well in the centre and add {{sunflower-oil}}, {{egg-yolks}}, 160 ml of water, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Whisk until smooth and well combined.

In a completely clean bowl, whisk {{egg-whites}} with {{cream-of-tartar}} until foamy. Gradually add the remaining 70 g of {{caster-sugar}} and whisk to stiff peaks. Fold one third of the whites into the batter to loosen it. Add the remaining whites and fold gently until just combined, using a large metal spoon. Work quickly and do not overfold.

Pour into the ungreased tube or bundt tin. Bake for 50–55 minutes until the surface is deep golden, the cake has risen above the rim, and a skewer comes out clean. Immediately invert the tin onto a rack and leave to cool upside-down for at least 60 minutes. To unmould: run a palette knife around the outside and inside edges, then lift out.
Any fat, even a trace of yolk, on the bowl or whisk prevents the whites reaching stiff peaks. Use a clean metal bowl and check the whisk.
Serve plain, dusted with icing sugar, or with whipped cream and fresh fruit. The light crumb can handle acidic fruit that would make a heavier cake soggy.
For an orange chiffon: replace the water and vanilla with 160 ml of fresh orange juice and the finely grated zest of two oranges.
